name: Advanced Graphs
concept:
## The idea

You've already done BFS/DFS, topo sort, and union-find. **Advanced graphs** means weighted edges and answering optimization questions: shortest path, minimum spanning tree, or reconstructing a path that uses every edge exactly once (Eulerian path).

**Dijkstra** (with a min-heap) finds the shortest path from a source to all nodes when all edge weights are **non-negative**. Each iteration pulls the nearest unvisited node and relaxes its neighbors. **Bellman-Ford** is slower (O(VE)) but handles negative weights and can detect negative cycles. **Minimum spanning tree** (MST) connects all nodes with the lowest total edge weight — Prim's is Dijkstra-shaped (grow from one node), Kruskal's uses union-find (add edges by weight, skip cycles). **Eulerian path** visits every *edge* exactly once — you reconstruct the path backward using DFS and edge removal (Hierholzer's algorithm).

These aren't fundamentally new ideas — they're variations on greedy + heap (Dijkstra, Prim), DP on edges (Bellman-Ford), or union-find (Kruskal).

## Python you'll pick up here

- `import heapq` and `heapq.heappush(heap, (priority, node))`, `heapq.heappop(heap)` — Python's min-heap
- `collections.defaultdict(list)` — adjacency lists that auto-create empty lists
- `float('inf')` for initialization of distances
- `(cost, node)` tuples — the heap compares by the first element (cost)
- List comprehensions and `enumerate` for building graph structures
- `.pop()` to remove the last element from a list (for Eulerian path reconstruction)
whenToUse: You need the shortest path in a weighted graph (Dijkstra if no negative weights, Bellman-Ford otherwise)
You need to connect all nodes with minimum total edge weight (MST — Prim or Kruskal)
You need to detect negative cycles or relax edges repeatedly
You need to visit every edge exactly once (Eulerian path/circuit)

problems.title: Min Cost to Connect All Points

video.title: NeetCode — Min Cost to Connect All Points (Python)

problems.keyIdeas: A **minimum spanning tree (MST)** problem. You have *n* nodes (points) and need to connect all with minimum total edge weight (Manhattan distance). Prim's algorithm is Dijkstra-shaped: start from any node, greedily add the cheapest edge to an unvisited node, repeat until all nodes are connected. O(E log V) with a heap.
hints: You need to connect all points with minimum total cost — that's the definition of a minimum spanning tree.
Prim's algorithm: start from any point, keep a min-heap of (cost, point) for edges to unvisited points, and greedily expand the tree.
Manhattan distance between (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) is `abs(x1 - x2) + abs(y1 - y2)`.

solutions.label: Optimal — Prim's MST
solutions.keyIdea: Start from any point, greedily add the cheapest edge to a new point using a min-heap.

solutions.label: Alternative — Kruskal's MST with union-find
solutions.keyIdea: Sort all edges by weight, then greedily add edges that don't create a cycle using union-find. Reach for Kruskal's when you naturally have all edges upfront (vs. Prim's which grows the tree node-by-node), or when union-find fits your mental model better. Both are O(E log E) time, but Kruskal's is cleaner when E is manageable.

problems.description: You are given an array points representing integer coordinates of some points on a 2D-plane, where points[i] = [xi, yi].

problems.title: Network Delay Time
problems.keyIdeas: This is **Dijkstra's shortest path** — find the minimum time for a signal to reach all nodes from a source. Initialize all distances to infinity except the source (0). Use a min-heap to always process the nearest unvisited node, and relax its neighbors. If all nodes are reachable, return the max distance; otherwise -1.
hints: You need the shortest time from source k to all other nodes — classic single-source shortest path.
Dijkstra: keep a min-heap of (time, node), always process the node with the smallest time, update neighbors if you find a shorter path.
After visiting all reachable nodes, the answer is the maximum distance in your distance array (or -1 if any node is unreachable).

solutions.label: Optimal — Dijkstra with min-heap
solutions.keyIdea: Greedily expand from the nearest node, relaxing neighbors. Track distances and return the max.

solutions.label: Alternative — Bellman-Ford
solutions.keyIdea: Relax all edges V-1 times to find shortest paths. Reach for Bellman-Ford when edges might be negative (not here, but it still works) or when you want a simpler implementation without a heap. Slower O(VE) vs Dijkstra's O(E log V), but handles negative weights and easier to understand.

problems.description: You are given a network of n nodes, labeled from 1 to n. You are also given times, a list of travel times as directed edges times[i] = (ui, vi, wi), where ui is the source node, vi is the target node, and wi is the time it takes for a signal to travel from source to target.

problems.title: Cheapest Flights Within K Stops

video.title: NeetCode — Cheapest Flights Within K Stops (Python)

problems.keyIdeas: This is **Dijkstra with a twist**: you can't always skip a node once visited, because a longer path (more stops) might unlock a cheaper route later. Store `(cost, node, stops)` in the heap, and track the minimum cost to reach each node *for each stop count*. Alternatively, use **Bellman-Ford** (relax edges k+1 times) — simpler but slower.
hints: Standard Dijkstra won't work because you need to track both cost and number of stops — a cheaper path might use too many stops.
Modified Dijkstra: push (cost, node, stops) into the heap. Track the best cost to each node for each stop count.
Alternatively, Bellman-Ford: relax all edges k+1 times (each iteration allows one more stop).

solutions.label: Better — Bellman-Ford (simpler)
solutions.keyIdea: Relax all edges k+1 times. After i iterations, you have the cheapest path using at most i stops.

solutions.label: Optimal — Modified Dijkstra
solutions.keyIdea: Heap stores (cost, node, stops). Only process if this path might improve future routes. Reach for this when you need the absolute best performance and can track state per (node, stops) — faster than Bellman-Ford when k is large.

solutions.label: Alternative — BFS with levels
solutions.keyIdea: BFS naturally tracks levels (stops), so process the graph level-by-level, updating the minimum cost to each node. Reach for BFS when the stop constraint dominates the problem and you want the simplest code — it's intuitive (each BFS level = one more stop) but slower O(k * E) than Dijkstra.
solutions.code:
class Solution:
    def findCheapestPrice(self, n: int, flights: list[list[int]], src: int, dst: int, k: int) -> int:
        from collections import defaultdict, deque
        # Build adjacency list
        graph = defaultdict(list)
        for u, v, price in flights:
            graph[u].append((v, price))
        
        # BFS with levels (each level = one stop)
        queue = deque([(src, 0)])  # (node, cost)
        min_cost = [float('inf')] * n
        min_cost[src] = 0
        stops = 0
        
        while queue and stops <= k:
            size = len(queue)
            for _ in range(size):
                node, cost = queue.popleft()
                for neighbor, price in graph[node]:
                    new_cost = cost + price
                    # Only explore if this is a better cost to this neighbor
                    if new_cost < min_cost[neighbor]:
                        min_cost[neighbor] = new_cost
                        queue.append((neighbor, new_cost))
            stops += 1
        
        return min_cost[dst] if min_cost[dst] != float('inf') else -1

problems.description: There are n cities connected by some number of flights. You are given an array flights where flights[i] = [fromi, toi, pricei] indicates that there is a flight from city fromi to city toi with cost pricei.

problems.title: Reconstruct Itinerary
problems.keyIdeas: This is an **Eulerian path** problem — visit every flight (edge) exactly once. Build an adjacency list sorted lexicographically, then use DFS to consume edges. The trick: you reconstruct the path **backward** (postorder), because Hierholzer's algorithm adds a node to the result *after* exhausting all its outgoing edges.
hints: You're asked to use every ticket exactly once — that's visiting every *edge* once, not every node. Think Eulerian path.
Build a graph where each airport maps to a sorted list of destinations. DFS from 'JFK', consuming edges as you go.
Hierholzer's algorithm: recursively visit all neighbors first, then add the current node to the result. Reverse the result at the end to get the forward path.

solutions.label: Optimal — Eulerian path (Hierholzer)
solutions.keyIdea: Use DFS with edge removal and postorder insertion. The path is built backward, then reversed.

problems.description: You are given a list of airline tickets where tickets[i] = [fromi, toi] represent the departure and the arrival airports of one flight. Reconstruct the itinerary in order and return it.

problems.title: Swim in Rising Water
problems.keyIdeas: This is a **modified Dijkstra** where the 'cost' is the maximum elevation along the path (not the sum). At each step, expand to the neighbor with the smallest elevation you haven't visited yet. Use a min-heap to always pick the lowest-elevation next cell. The answer is the elevation you had to wait for to reach the bottom-right.
hints: You want the path where the maximum elevation along it is as small as possible — this is a shortest-path problem with a twist.
Use Dijkstra, but instead of summing weights, track the maximum elevation seen so far on the path.
Start from (0, 0) with the elevation grid[0][0], and greedily expand to the lowest available neighbor.

solutions.label: Optimal — Modified Dijkstra
solutions.keyIdea: Use a min-heap to always expand to the cell with the smallest max-elevation-so-far.

solutions.label: Alternative — Binary search + DFS
solutions.keyIdea: Binary search on the answer (the time T we wait), then for each candidate T, check if a path exists using only cells with elevation ≤ T via DFS. Reach for this when the problem asks 'minimize the maximum' and you can verify a candidate answer quickly. Same O(n^2 log n) complexity but conceptually different — search the answer space instead of path-finding.

solutions.label: Alternative — Union-Find
solutions.keyIdea: Sort cells by elevation, then union adjacent cells in increasing order until start and end are connected. Reach for union-find when the problem is about connectivity and you can process elements in a favorable order. Elegant but less intuitive for this problem — most would reach for Dijkstra or binary search first.

problems.description: You are given an n x n integer matrix grid where each value grid[i][j] represents the elevation at that point (i, j).

problems.title: Alien Dictionary
problems.keyIdeas: Extract ordering constraints by comparing adjacent words pairwise (the first differing character gives an edge). Build a graph and run **topological sort** (DFS with postorder) to get the character order. Detect cycles (impossible ordering). This is a graph problem disguised as a string problem — the trick is extracting the edges correctly.
hints: Compare each pair of adjacent words. The first position where they differ tells you one character comes before another — that's a directed edge.
Build a graph of characters and their ordering constraints, then do a topological sort to find a valid order.
Use DFS-based topo sort with a postorder stack. If you find a cycle (revisit a node in the current path), return '' — the ordering is impossible.

solutions.label: Optimal — Topological sort (DFS)
solutions.keyIdea: Extract edges from adjacent words, build a graph, then DFS-based topo sort with cycle detection.

solutions.label: Alternative — Topological sort (BFS/Kahn's)
solutions.keyIdea: Build a graph with in-degrees, then repeatedly remove nodes with in-degree 0 and decrement their neighbors' in-degrees. Reach for BFS topo sort (Kahn's algorithm) when you prefer iterative code or need to process nodes in a specific order as they become available. Same O(C) complexity as DFS, but more explicit about the order.
